perturbation.original_text: 6.1.3 A term or condition of this Agr eement can be waived or modified only by written consent of both parties. No fa ilure or delay in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operat e as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: CLAUSE 5. NO IMPLIED OBLIGATIONS 6.1.3 A term or condition of this Agr eement can be waived or modified only by written consent of both parties. No fa ilure or delay in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operat e as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the "waiver and modification" clause to the section on "no implied obligations" obscures its importance and general applicability to all terms of the contract. This implies that the waiver requirements only apply to the obligations described in that section.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sale of Goods Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.1, s. 13
perturbation.law_citation: Sale of Goods Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.1, s. 13
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Sale of Goods Act concerns implied conditions as to quality or fitness. Moving the modification clause to the “No Implied Obligations” section implies that the written modification requirement only applies to these types of obligations related to sale of goods, and not other obligations, which is incorrect.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a structural flaw that introduces a legal contradiction. The original contract has a general waiver and modification clause, applicable to all terms. The change moves this clause into a section labeled "No Implied Obligations." As explained, this obscures the importance and general applicability of the clause, creating the implication that the waiver requirements only apply to implied obligations. The Law Explanation supports this, saying that it incorrectly implies the written modification requirement only applies to obligations related to the sale of goods, and not other obligations. The first snippet mentions section 13, Implied Conditions and Warranties, of the Sale of Goods Act which supports the LLM's reasoning that this change could be interpreted as only applying to the 'implied' conditions.
